content:
I am wondering if one can get in work in halfway sane way to pass a small Rust closure to the kind of C callback API we see sometimes in the kernel without having to allocate heap memory.

The kind of API I talk about looks like this:

struct CObjectWithCallback {
    ...
    void (*callback_func)(void *);
    void* user_data;
}

The question is, if the size of the closure is not more than the size of a pointer, could we store the closure somehow in the user_data pointer directly - to avoid the memory allocation - instead of boxing it and setting user_data to Box::into_raw(closure).

It looks to me that would be useful in kernel to do callbacks 'the rust way'.

Unfortunately it seems using mem::transmute for that can result in UB.

We had a discussion about the matter (without finding an elegant solution) here:

https://users.rust-lang.org/t/is-transmuting-a-value-to-a-pointer-and-back-safe/126296

The TLDR is, if the data (here: the closure) contains padding between values, like if it contains an u16 and an u32, llvm treats the padding as unitialized memory. There seems to be no way to make the compiler assume it to be initialized in Rust (without knowing the exact layout of the data, which is impossible for a closure).

I wonder if it would be worth the trouble to make that work anyway somehow, like with some C or assembly helpers. Would that feature be generally useful enough? Thoughts?

I'm a bit confused, you're talking about storing a closure, but user_data is just avoid*. What kinds of closures do you think this would apply to? If you're not capturing any data, you can cast a closure to a fn pointer, which you can store as the void* (it will be "statically allocated" like any other normal function).

Benno Lossin schrieb:

I'm a bit confused, you're talking about storing a closure, but user_data is just avoid*. What kinds of closures do you think this would apply to?

I mean, if the closure captures something simple, small, that is not larger than a pointer, like a an u64 or a Box. I mean physically a pointer are 8 bytes (or 4 on 32bit), so in there is space to store anything that is <= 8 bytes in size.
do you have some concrete examples?

Benno Lossin schrieb:

do you have some concrete examples?

You mean use case or implementation?

My current use case is the completion callback of a bio. There you have

pub type bio_end_io_t = ::core::option::Option<unsafe extern "C" fn(arg1: *mut bio)>;

struct bio {
    ...
    pub bi_end_io: bio_end_io_t,
    pub bi_private: *mut ffi::c_void,
    ...

You get the bio passed to the callback and can get the closure back from bi_private.

Currently I'm doing it like this:

struct Bio {
    ...
    unsafe extern "C" fn callback_wrapper<CB: FnOnce(Bio) + Send + 'static>(bio: *mut bindings::bio) {
        unsafe {
            let cb = KBox::from_raw(bio.read().bi_private as *mut CB);
            let submit_bio = Self(NonNull::new_unchecked(bio));
            KBox::into_inner(cb)(submit_bio);
        }
    }
    pub fn submit_noacct<CB: FnOnce(Bio) + Send + 'static>(self, end_io_callback: CB) -> Result {
        let bio = mem::ManuallyDrop::new(self).as_raw_bio();
        let cb: unsafe extern "C" fn(bio: *mut bindings::bio) = Self::callback_wrapper::<CB>;
        let end_io_callback = KBox::into_raw(KBox::new(end_io_callback, alloc::flags::GFP_NOIO)?);
        unsafe{
            (*bio).bi_end_io = Some(cb);
            (*bio).bi_private = end_io_callback as *const c_void as *mut c_void;
            bindings::submit_bio_noacct(bio);
        }
        Ok(())
    }
    ...
}

As you can see, I allocate a KBox on each call to submit_noacct(). What I would like to do is something like:

    if std::mem::size_of::<T>() <= std::mem::size_of::<*mut c_void>() {
        // UB, depending on what the closure captures
        let end_io_callback = core::mem::transmute<_, *mut c_void>(end_io_callback);
    } else {
        let end_io_callback = KBox::into_raw(KBox::new(end_io_callback, alloc::flags::GFP_NOIO)?);
    }

It probably can be made sound with assembly or C, but correctness will be hard to check (without in-depth knowledge of the compiler). Thus the question if it is worth the trouble.

Oliver Mangold said:

You mean use case or implementation?

I mean the use-case.

Thanks for the details, how do the submit_noacct call sites look like?

Benno Lossin schrieb:

Thanks for the details, how do the submit_noacct call sites look like?

No really clear. It's supposed to be a generic interface.

Sure, I could tailor it to my specific use cases, where I typically need to store an int, Box or ARef. No capturing closures, only func pointers with a fixed argument type. That would be plan B.

If you don't pull end_io_callback in a box, it may be dropped after submit_noacct() returns, e.g.

{
  let y = ...;
  let _ = bio.submit_noacct(move || { y.do_sth(); ... });
}
// the closure above gets dropped and so does `y`.
// <callback_wrapper> will reference a dangling pointer

Boqun Feng schrieb:

If you don't pull end_io_callback in a box, it may be dropped after submit_noacct() returns

But that should be possible to handle with ManuallyDrop, no?

Boqun Feng said:

If you don't pull end_io_callback in a box, it may be dropped after submit_noacct() returns, e.g.

{
  let y = ...;
  let _ = bio.submit_noacct(move || { y.do_sth(); ... });
}
// the closure above gets dropped and so does `y`.
// <callback_wrapper> will reference a dangling pointer

that can't happen, since CB has to be 'static
Oh, my bad. Shouldn't use word "dropped", if we don't move the closure in Box, the pointer will become dangling when the calling function stack popped.
which pointer?

Benno Lossin said:

which pointer?

The pointer to the closure, that Oliver wants to store in bio.

Boqun Feng schrieb:

The pointer to the closure, that Oliver wants to store in bio.

It shouldn't. The closure will be moved into the function with normal move semantics. When dropping is prevented there using ManuallyDrop it should be fine.

Oliver Mangold said:

Boqun Feng schrieb:

The pointer to the closure, that Oliver wants to store in bio.

It shouldn't. The closure will be moved into the function with normal move semantics. When dropping is prevented there using ManuallyDrop it should be fine.

If you don't use Box, where the closure should be? On the stack, right?

fn foo(bio: &mut Bio) {
  let y = ...;
  let _ = bio.submit_noacct(move || { y.do_sth(); ... });
  // both y and the closure should be allocated on the stack of `foo()`.
}

foo(bio);
// y and the closure are not valid. yet the callback may have not executed.
that doesn't work because of the 'static bound

Benno Lossin said:

that doesn't work because of the 'static bound

which doesn't work? I thought Oliver was proposing to use ManuallyDrop
but it might be implementation detail
Yeah..

Boqun Feng schrieb:

If you don't use Box, where the closure should be? On the stack, right?

Wherever it is moved. The same way as you pass primitives or structs around. The idea here is to store it in the bi_private field (after checking that it fits)-

Oliver Mangold said:

Boqun Feng schrieb:

If you don't use Box, where the closure should be? On the stack, right?

Wherever it is moved. The same way as you pass primitives or structs around. The idea here is to store it in the bi_private field (after checking that it fits)-

move to where?

Boqun Feng said:

Benno Lossin said:

that doesn't work because of the 'static bound

which doesn't work? I thought Oliver was proposing to use ManuallyDrop

in your example the closure will be moved into the submit_noacct function and stay valid for as long as it exists

Boqun Feng said:

Oliver Mangold said:

Boqun Feng schrieb:

If you don't use Box, where the closure should be? On the stack, right?

Wherever it is moved. The same way as you pass primitives or structs around. The idea here is to store it in the bi_private field (after checking that it fits)-

move to where?

Or you mean bi_private should be able to hold the whole closure?
yes that is the idea
if we store the data inside of an Opaque<CB>, then it would work
but no idea how to bring that into C land...
correct me if I'm wrong, a closure is at least a size of usize because it has a function pointer in it?
nope, if you have a 'static closure, then it can be smaller

for example:

fn size<F: FnOnce() -> i32 + 'static>(f: F) {
    println!("{}", size_of::<F>());
}

fn foo(x: i32) {
    size(move || x);
}

fn main() {
    foo(42);
}

prints 4.
(and the binary representation of that closure will just be its captured data, so in this case 42)
Ok, this makes sense.

Benno Lossin said:

if we store the data inside of an Opaque<CB>, then it would work

what do you mean by this?
if the bi_private field had the type Opaque<usize>, then we could store CB in there without padding bytes becoming an issue
Oh, right. Because whatever the type of bi_private is, it needs to be valid.

I guess if we have a __opaque on C side

#define __opaque __attribute__((???))

and bindgen can understand it. Generating void *bi_private __opaque into a bi_private: Opaque<*mut c_void>, then this could work.

struct bio {
    ...
    void *bi_private __opaque;
    ...
}

into

struct Bio {
    ...
    bi_private: Opaque<*mut c_void>,
    ...
}

Benno Lossin schrieb:

if the bi_private field had the type Opaque<usize>, then we could store CB in there without padding bytes becoming an issue

According to the forum discussion I linked, it seems not. If it were an Opaque<[u8; 8]> it might. Not sure.

With an Opaque<usize>, converting the closure into that should be okay, but when you want to get the data back, you have a problem. Some bytes of the usize being uninitialized will make the compiler declare the whole usize to be uninitialized.

you can probably do a:

let cb = unsafe { (bio.bi_private.get() as *mut CB).read() };
cb();

, that is, don't read it as a unsize.
https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=64adacc3f2c393fce19d05c5364de975
(it makes miri happy)

Boqun Feng schrieb:

https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=64adacc3f2c393fce19d05c5364de975

Interesting. Works also for me, if I replace usize with *mut c_void.
Now bindgen would need to be taught to generate MaybeUninit<*mut c_void> instead of *mut c_void...
Hmm.. your Bio is a Opaque<bindings::bio>, right?

Boqun Feng schrieb:

Hmm.. your Bio is a Opaque<bindings::bio>, right?

Yes. Does that help?

then you could do:

    if std::mem::size_of::<CB>() <= std::mem::size_of::<*mut c_void>() {
        unsafe { addr_mut_of!((*self.0.get()).bi_private).write(end_io_callback); }
        forget(end_io_callback);
    } else {
        <do the normal box thing>
    }
the whole point is "don't read .bi_private directly, instead re-interpret it as a CB".
and you probably need to check align of CB is smaller or equal than *mut c_void just in case.

Boqun Feng schrieb:

the whole point is "don't read .bi_private directly, instead re-interpret it as a CB".

Good idea. That make sense. If it is effectively never accesses as a *c_void it might indeed be okay.

Boqun Feng schrieb:

and you probably need to check align of CB is smaller or equal than *mut c_void just in case.

Is it actually possible, that it is not? If the size of CB is not greater than a pointer?

Oliver Mangold said:

Boqun Feng schrieb:

and you probably need to check align of CB is smaller or equal than *mut c_void just in case.

Is it actually possible, that it is not? If the size of CB is not greater than a pointer?

No for right now, but these are compile time switches only, so it won't hurt being careful.

Boqun Feng schrieb:

No for right now, but these are compile time switches only, so it won't hurt being careful.

True.

But seems you indeed found a solution. Thanks a lot!

Oliver Mangold said:

Boqun Feng schrieb:

Hmm.. your Bio is a Opaque<bindings::bio>, right?

Yes. Does that help?

:man_facepalming: oh right!
@Oliver Mangold do you really need to be able to pass a closure here?
Could the callback not be decided by a generic parameter on Bio ?

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

Could the callback not be decided by a generic parameter on Bio ?

Basically that's what I'm doing. But the question is about passing how to pass user_data to the callback in safe code.

If doing things like above is not okay, I could still make it usable by only allowing a fixed set of types that can be used for that (like an usize or a Box). That said, being able to use closures seems to me to be most flexible way and also the common Rust idiom for callbacks, no?
We do something similar for tag set private data. There we require foreignOwnable for the parameter I think, and then it is generic.
As long as you always have the same type on a code path, that works well, it can be generic.

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

As long as you always have the same type on a code path, that works well, it can be generic.

Yes, it would. Don't get me wrong, I'm not saying closures are absolutely required.
I am not sure how the codegen works, but I guess you would always have some kind of pointer deref, if you use a closure?

Andreas Hindborg said:

I am not sure how the codegen works, but I guess you would always have some kind of pointer deref, if you use a closure?

Nope, compiler should figure if the data captured by the closure is relatively small.

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

I am not sure how the codegen works, but I guess you would always have some kind of pointer deref, if you use a closure?

As I understand the value of the closure is just the captured data, somehow packed into a kinda struct. How that struct is passed around is decided by the compiler, of course. But I think memcpy'ing it to move it is just as valid a thing to do as for any other struct. The complication is just the type coercion if you want to store it in the space dedicated for data of a different type with causing UB. (The compiler surely won't convert a boxing into inline storage for you, if the reference to the Box is an FFI pointer, right?). But I believe Boqun figured that part out.
I guess some times it will be a fat pointer? A pointer for the function to jump to and a pointer to the captures?
Unless it is static dispatch, both are required

Boqun Feng said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I am not sure how the codegen works, but I guess you would always have some kind of pointer deref, if you use a closure?

Nope, compiler should figure if the data captured by the closure is relatively small.

But is it possible to put a bound on this? To accept only closures that will fit in a usize?

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

I guess some times it will be a fat pointer? A pointer for the function to jump to and a pointer to the captures?

No fat pointer in the regular sense. The function address is represented by the _type_ of the closure, while the capture is represented by it's value. Odd thing, I admit. If you want to have both addresses, you can get the function address by converting the closure to a function pointer and the data address by simply dereferencing the closure. I did that in my code here.

pub fn submit_noacct<CB: FnOnce(Bio) + Send + 'static>(self, end_io_callback: CB) -> Result {
        let bio = mem::ManuallyDrop::new(self).as_raw_bio();
        let cb: unsafe extern "C" fn(bio: *mut bindings::bio) = Self::callback_wrapper::<CB>;
        let end_io_callback = KBox::into_raw(KBox::new(end_io_callback, alloc::flags::GFP_NOIO)?);
        unsafe{
            (*bio).bi_end_io = Some(cb);
            (*bio).bi_private = end_io_callback as *const c_void as *mut c_void;
            bindings::submit_bio_noacct(bio);
        }
        Ok(())
    }

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

But is it possible to put a bound on this? To accept only closures that will fit in a usize?

That's what I did. You can do mem::size_of::<C>() on a closure just like on any other type.

Oliver Mangold said:

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

But is it possible to put a bound on this? To accept only closures that will fit in a usize?

That's what I did. You can do mem::size_of::<C>() on a closure just like on any other type.

Right. What I meant was, is it possible to restrict the closures to only be he kind that fits in a usize, with a trait bound?
Maybe it is a stupid thing to do, but I am curious.

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

Right. What I meant was, is it possible to restrict the closures to only be he kind that fits in a usize, with a trait bound?

Don't think so. But one can do the size check in a const section, producing a compile time error when it doesn't fit.
